interest: Capable/middle-class families seeking early ability grouping and non-state (church/foundation) maintainers protecting their institutional autonomy and differentiated mission.
value: Family liberty/subsidiarity and excellence (early talent identification) in tension with system-level equity.
fear: That abolition strips families of the choice to advance capable children early and forces non-state maintainers into an unchosen role as residual-sorting repositories, causing reputational damage and mission-drift.
affected: State comprehensive schools receiving heterogeneous cohorts
Teachers required to deliver differentiated instruction
assumption: That early merit-based selection genuinely identifies talent rather than SES advantage, and that family/maintainer choice is a liberty to protect rather than a mechanism of stratification.
empirical_uncertainty: Contested and weak: the Hanushek & Woessmann cross-national tracking/SES relationship does not settle whether removing selection reduces SES gradients in the Hungarian context, and the scenario itself flags middle-class exit to choice/non-state channels as the largest risk, so whether sorting is reduced or merely relocated is unknown.
